_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: Medical Tourism Hit RM2.72B in 2024, Targeting RM3B in 2025, While Public Specialists Stretch Thin
context: MHTC reported RM2.72 billion in 2024 medical-tourism revenue from 1.6 million healthcare travellers and projects RM3 billion in 2025. The 2030 target is RM12 billion. Public hospitals continue to face specialist shortages.

cards.text: Medical tourism earned RM2.72 billion in 2024, targeting RM12 billion by 2030, while public hospitals queue patients for specialist care.
cards.sub: MHTC: 1.6 million healthcare travellers in 2024, +14% year-on-year. The 2025 target is RM3 billion.

cards.h: Most medical tourists are treated at the same private hospitals where the country's senior specialists concentrate.
cards.s: The Flagship Medical Tourism Hospital programme recognises Institut Jantung Negara, Island Hospital Penang, Mahkota Medical Centre and Subang Jaya Medical Centre.
cards.d: Most medical tourists are treated at the same private hospitals where the country's senior specialists concentrate. The Flagship Medical Tourism Hospital programme recognises Institut Jantung Negara, Island Hospital Penang, Mahkota Medical Centre and Subang Jaya Medical Centre.

cards.h: MHTC's 2030 target is roughly four times the 2024 actual, implying continued specialist concentration in private hospitals.
cards.s: Growth targets compete with public healthcare human-resource needs unless specialist supply also expands.
cards.d: MHTC's 2030 target is roughly four times the 2024 actual, implying continued specialist concentration in private hospitals. Growth targets compete with public healthcare human-resource needs unless specialist supply also expands.

cards.h: Other countries with active medical tourism use cross-subsidy mechanisms to ensure local patients benefit.
cards.s: India's Charitable Hospital obligations require some private hospitals to reserve beds for low-income local patients. Malaysia has no equivalent mandate.
cards.d: Other countries with active medical tourism use cross-subsidy mechanisms to ensure local patients benefit. India's Charitable Hospital obligations require some private hospitals to reserve beds for low-income local patients. Malaysia has no equivalent mandate.

cards.text: The growth story is real. The structural question is whether the specialist supply expands fast enough that public-sector waits do not lengthen as exports rise.
Like a restaurant that serves tourists a five-course meal while the kitchen staff eat leftovers: same building, different service tiers.
cards.sub: Without parallel investment in specialist training, every export patient has an opportunity cost in the public queue.

cards.text: Medical tourism is legitimate. The fix is to expand specialist supply alongside it, and to require local cross-subsidy at high-volume hospitals.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
